title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.2 Matrix norms
To measure a matrix we ask how much it can stretch a vector. Any function \(
orm{\cdot}:\R^{n	imes n}	o\R\) satisfying nonnegativity/definiteness, homogeneity, and the triangle inequality is a candidate, but matrices compose, so we demand one more property.

Definition 5.2 (Matrix norm) A matrix norm on \(\R^{n	imes n}\) is a function \(
orm{\cdot}\) obeying the three vector-norm axioms together with submultiplicativity, \(
orm{AB}\le
orm{A}\,
orm{B}\).

The most useful matrix norms are those induced by a vector norm: they measure the largest factor by which \(A\) can lengthen a unit vector.

Definition 5.3 (Induced (natural) matrix norm) Given a vector norm \(
orm{\cdot}\) on \(\R^n\), the induced (or natural) matrix norm is \[

orm{A}=\max_{
orm{x}=1}
orm{Ax}=\max_{z
e 0} rac{
orm{Az}}{
orm{z}} .
	ag{5.3}\]

The two forms in Equation 5.3 agree by homogeneity (\(z=x/
orm{x}\)). By its very definition an induced norm satisfies \(
orm{Az}\le
orm{A}\,
orm{z}\) for every \(z\), and this is what makes it submultiplicative: \(
orm{ABz}\le
orm{A}\,
orm{Bz}\le
orm{A}\,
orm{B}\,
orm{z}\) for all \(z\), so \(
orm{AB}\le
orm{A}\,
orm{B}\) (and the bound chains, e.g. \(
orm{ABCz}\le
orm{A}
orm{B}
orm{C}
orm{z}\)). We write \(
orm{A}_p\) for the norm induced by the vector \(p\)-norm. The \(\infty\)-norm has a clean closed form as the maximum absolute row sum.

Theorem 5.3 (Infinity matrix norm) For \(A=(a_{ij})\), \(\;\displaystyle
inf{A}=\max_{1\le i\le n}\sum_{j=1}^n\abs{a_{ij}}\).


Proof. Set \(C=\max_i\sum_j\abs{a_{ij}}\). For any \(x\) with \(
inf{x}\le 1\), \[

inf{Ax}=\max_i\Bigl|\sum_j a_{ij}x_j\Bigr|\le\max_i\sum_j\abs{a_{ij}}\,\abs{x_j}
\le\max_i\sum_j\abs{a_{ij}}\;
inf{x}=C\,
inf{x},
\] so \(
inf{A}\le C\). For the reverse, let \(i\) be a row attaining the maximum and set \(x_j=\operatorname{sign}(a_{ij})\), a unit \(\infty\)-norm vector; then the \(i\)-th component of \(Ax\) is \(\sum_j a_{ij}\operatorname{sign}(a_{ij})=\sum_j\abs{a_{ij}}=C\), so \(
inf{Ax}\ge C\) and hence \(
inf{A}\ge C\). Therefore \(
inf{A}=C\). \(\square\)

The Euclidean matrix norm has no such elementary formula; it is the largest singular value, \[

two{A}=\sqrt{\spec(A^	op A)}=\sigma_1(A),
	ag{5.4}\] where \(\spec(\cdot)\) is the spectral radius of the next section. To see why, note \(
two{A}^2=\max_{
orm{x}=1}x^	op(A^	op A)x\); by the Courant–Fischer maximum principle applied to the symmetric positive-semidefinite matrix \(A^	op A\) this maximum is its largest eigenvalue, and since an SVD \(A=U\Sigma V^	op\) gives \(A^	op A=V\Sigma^2 V^	op\), that eigenvalue is \(\sigma_1(A)^2\). We take Equation 5.4 on faith here; the eigen/singular-value machinery behind it is developed in Chapter 7.






Important



Not every “matrix norm” is induced. Stacking the entries of \(A\) into a long vector and applying a vector \(p\)-norm inherits every axiom except submultiplicativity — except in one case, the Frobenius norm \(
orm{A}_F=(\sum_{ij}a_{ij}^2)^{1/2}=\sqrt{	r(A^	op A)}\), which is submultiplicative and hence a genuine matrix norm, yet is not induced by any vector norm. In particular \(
orm{A}_F
e
two{A}\); do not conflate them.

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.3 Spectral radius
Convergence of every stationary iteration below is governed by a single number attached to a matrix: the largest magnitude of its eigenvalues. Recall \(\lambda\in\C\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) if \(Ax=\lambda x\) for some \(x\ne 0\); the eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic polynomial \(\det(A-\lambda I)\) and may be complex even for real \(A\).

Definition 5.4 (Spectral radius) The spectral radius of \(A\in\R^{n\times n}\) is \[
\spec(A)=\max\bigl\{\,\abs{\lambda}\;:\;\lambda\text{ an eigenvalue of }A\,\bigr\},
\] the maximum taken over all (possibly complex) eigenvalues.

The spectral radius is never larger than any induced norm, and it is the exact rate at which powers of \(A\) grow or decay.

Proposition 5.1 (Spectral radius bounds) For any induced matrix norm, \(\spec(A)\le\norm{A}\).


Proof. Let \(\lambda\) attain the spectral radius, with eigenvector \(x\ne 0\), and let \(X\) be the matrix whose columns are all \(x\), so \(AX=\lambda X\) and \(X\ne 0\). Taking any induced norm and using homogeneity and submultiplicativity, \(\abs{\lambda}\,\norm{X}=\norm{AX}\le\norm{A}\,\norm{X}\); dividing by \(\norm{X}>0\) gives \(\abs{\lambda}\le\norm{A}\), hence \(\spec(A)\le\norm{A}\). If \(\lambda\) is complex the eigenvector \(x\) (and hence \(X\)) is complex; the same estimate goes through verbatim once the induced norm is extended to \(\C^{n\times n}\) in the usual way, so no generality is lost. \(\square\)


Definition 5.5 (Convergent matrix) A matrix \(A\) is convergent if \(\lim_{k\to\infty}(A^k)_{ij}=0\) for all \(i,j\), equivalently (by equivalence of norms on \(\R^{n\times n}\)) if \(\norm{A^k}\to 0\) for some — hence every — matrix norm.

The following equivalence is the analytic heart of the chapter: it converts “the iteration converges” into the checkable condition \(\spec(T)<1\).

Theorem 5.4 (Characterization of convergent matrices) For \(A\in\R^{n\times n}\) the following are equivalent: (i) \(A\) is convergent; (ii) \(\lim_{k\to\infty}\norm{A^k}=0\) for some (equivalently every) matrix norm; (iii) \(\spec(A)<1\); (iv) \(\lim_{k\to\infty}A^k x=0\) for every \(x\in\R^n\).


Proof. Equivalence of (i), (ii), (iv) is the equivalence of norms applied to the vectorized powers, together with the observations that (iv)\(\Rightarrow\)(i) by plugging in the standard basis vectors \(e_j\), and (i)\(\Rightarrow\)(iv) by expanding \(A^kx\) coordinatewise and taking limits. That \(\spec(A)<1\) is equivalent to convergence is the standard spectral criterion (via the Jordan form, \(\norm{A^k}\to 0\) iff every eigenvalue has modulus \(<1\)); we take this direction as known. The one-line intuition: if some \(\abs{\lambda}\ge 1\) then \(A^k\) does not shrink the corresponding eigenvector, while if all \(\abs{\lambda}<1\) the powers decay geometrically at rate \(\spec(A)\). \(\square\)
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.4 The Jacobi method
We now build the first iteration for \(Ax=b\). Split \(A\) into its diagonal, strictly lower, and strictly upper triangular parts, \[
A=D-L-U,
\tag{5.5}\] where \(D=\diag(a_{11},\dots,a_{nn})\), \(-L\) is the strictly lower part, and \(-U\) the strictly upper part. (Beware: these \(L,U\) are not the factors of an \(LU\) factorization from Chapter 4; they are fixed pieces of \(A\), not computed.) Assume every diagonal entry \(a_{ii}\ne 0\), so \(D\) is invertible.
Rewriting \(Ax=b\) as \((D-L-U)x=b\), i.e. \(Dx=b+(L+U)x\), exhibits the solution as a fixed point of the map \[
F(x)=D^{-1}\bigl[b+(L+U)x\bigr] .
\] The Jacobi method is the fixed-point iteration \(x^{(k+1)}=F(x^{(k)})\): pick \(x^{(0)}\) and iterate. If the iterates converge, continuity of \(F\) forces the limit to satisfy \(x^\star=F(x^\star)\), i.e. to solve \(Ax=b\). Whether they converge is the subject of Section 5.6.






NoteAlgorithm — Jacobi iteration



Given \(A\) (with \(a_{ii}\ne 0\)), \(b\), and \(x^{(0)}\), iterate for \(k=0,1,\dots\) \[
x^{(k+1)}=D^{-1}\bigl[b+(L+U)x^{(k)}\bigr],
\qquad\text{componentwise}\qquad
x_i^{(k+1)}=\frac{1}{a_{ii}}\Bigl(b_i-\sum_{\substack{j=1\\ j\ne i}}^{n}a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}\Bigr).
\tag{5.6}\] Each new component uses only old components \(x_j^{(k)}\), so the updates are independent and the sweep parallelizes trivially.
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.5 The Gauss–Seidel method
Jacobi computes an entire new vector from the old one. But while sweeping \(i=1,\dots,n\) we have already improved \(x_1^{(k+1)},\dots,x_{i-1}^{(k+1)}\) by the time we reach component \(i\) — why not use them? Grouping the splitting Equation 5.5 as \((D-L)x=b+Ux\) instead does exactly that, suggesting the iteration \[
x^{(k+1)}=(D-L)^{-1}\bigl[b+Ux^{(k)}\bigr],
\tag{5.7}\] the Gauss–Seidel method. Implementing it does not require inverting \(D-L\): it is the lower-triangular system \((D-L)x^{(k+1)}=b+Ux^{(k)}\), solved by forward substitution. Expanding the \(i\)-th equation \(a_{ii}x_i^{(k+1)}+\sum_{j<i}a_{ij}x_j^{(k+1)}=b_i-\sum_{j>i}a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}\) and solving for \(x_i^{(k+1)}\) gives the in-place formula.






NoteAlgorithm — Gauss–Seidel iteration



Given \(A\) (with \(a_{ii}\ne 0\)), \(b\), and \(x^{(0)}\), iterate for \(k=0,1,\dots\) and \(i=1,\dots,n\) in order \[
x_i^{(k+1)}=\frac{1}{a_{ii}}\Bigl(b_i-\sum_{j=1}^{i-1}a_{ij}x_j^{(k+1)}
-\sum_{j=i+1}^{n}a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}\Bigr).
\tag{5.8}\] The right-hand side uses the already updated \(x_j^{(k+1)}\) for \(j<i\), so components are computed sequentially with no extra work — like forward substitution for a triangular matrix.


Jacobi and Gauss–Seidel cost the same number of flops per iteration (ignoring parallelism); which one converges in fewer iterations has no clean general answer, though sufficient conditions for Gauss–Seidel to win are given by the Stein–Rosenberg theorem (see the textbook).
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.6 Convergence of Jacobi and Gauss–Seidel
Both iterations have the stationary form \[
x^{(k+1)}=Tx^{(k)}+c,
\tag{5.9}\] with iteration matrix \(T\) and constant \(c\) read off from Equation 5.6 and Equation 5.7: \[
\text{Jacobi:}\quad T=D^{-1}(L+U),\ \ c=D^{-1}b;\qquad
\text{Gauss–Seidel:}\quad T=(D-L)^{-1}U,\ \ c=(D-L)^{-1}b .
\tag{5.10}\] The convergence of any iteration of the form Equation 5.9 depends only on \(T\), through the spectral criterion of Theorem 5.4.

Theorem 5.5 (Convergence of stationary iterations) Let \(T\in\R^{n\times n}\) and \(c\in\R^n\). The iteration \(x^{(k+1)}=Tx^{(k)}+c\) converges to the unique solution of \(x=Tx+c\) for every starting point \(x^{(0)}\) and every \(c\) if and only if \[
\spec(T)<1 .
\]


Proof. Suppose \(\spec(T)<1\). Then \(1\) is not an eigenvalue of \(T\), so \(I-T\) is invertible and \(x=Tx+c\) has the unique solution \(x=(I-T)^{-1}c\). Define the error \(y^{(k)}=x^{(k)}-x\). Subtracting \(x=Tx+c\) from \(x^{(k+1)}=Tx^{(k)}+c\) gives \[
y^{(k+1)}=x^{(k+1)}-x=T x^{(k)}-Tx=T\,y^{(k)},
\] hence \(y^{(k)}=T^k y^{(0)}\). By Theorem 5.4, \(\spec(T)<1\) makes \(T\) convergent, so \(y^{(k)}=T^k y^{(0)}\to 0\), i.e. \(x^{(k)}\to x\) for every \(x^{(0)}\).
Conversely, suppose the iteration converges for every \(x^{(0)}\) and every \(c\). Take \(c=0\): the fixed point is \(x=0\) and \(x^{(k)}=T^k x^{(0)}\to 0\) for every \(x^{(0)}\), which is precisely condition (iv) of Theorem 5.4. Hence \(T\) is convergent and \(\spec(T)<1\). \(\square\)

When an induced norm of \(T\) is already below \(1\) we get convergence and a quantitative rate, directly from \(\spec(T)\le\norm{T}\) (Proposition 5.1).

Corollary 5.1 (Error estimates) If \(\norm{T}<1\) for some induced norm, the iteration converges for every \(x^{(0)}\), and \[
\norm{x^{(k)}-x}\le\norm{T}^{k}\,\norm{x^{(0)}-x},
\qquad
\norm{x^{(k)}-x}\le\frac{\norm{T}^{k}}{1-\norm{T}}\,\norm{x^{(1)}-x^{(0)}} .
\tag{5.11}\]


Proof. The first bound is \(\norm{y^{(k)}}=\norm{T^ky^{(0)}}\le\norm{T}^k\norm{y^{(0)}}\). For the second, telescope \(x^{(k)}-x^{(l)}=\sum_{j=0}^{l-k-1}\bigl(x^{(k+j)}-x^{(k+j+1)}\bigr)\) for \(l>k\) and use \(x^{(k+j)}-x^{(k+j+1)}=T^{k+j}(x^{(0)}-x^{(1)})\) to get \(\norm{x^{(k)}-x^{(l)}}\le\norm{T}^k\bigl(\sum_{j\ge 0}\norm{T}^j\bigr)\norm{x^{(1)}-x^{(0)}}\); letting \(l\to\infty\) and summing the geometric series \(\sum_j\norm{T}^j=1/(1-\norm{T})\) yields the claim. \(\square\)

For Jacobi in the \(\infty\)-norm this criterion becomes a condition on \(A\) alone. With \(T=D^{-1}(L+U)\) we have \(T_{ij}=-a_{ij}/a_{ii}\) for \(i\ne j\) and \(T_{ii}=0\), so by Theorem 5.3, \[
\ninf{T}=\max_i\sum_{j\ne i}\frac{\abs{a_{ij}}}{\abs{a_{ii}}}<1
\iff
\abs{a_{ii}}>\sum_{j\ne i}\abs{a_{ij}}\ \text{ for all }i .
\]

Definition 5.6 (Strict diagonal dominance) A matrix \(A=(a_{ij})\) is strictly diagonally dominant if \(\abs{a_{ii}}>\sum_{j\ne i}\abs{a_{ij}}\) for every \(i\).


Theorem 5.6 (Convergence under diagonal dominance) If \(A\) is strictly diagonally dominant, then for any \(b\) and any \(x^{(0)}\) both the Jacobi and the Gauss–Seidel iterations converge to the solution of \(Ax=b\).


Proof. For Jacobi the computation above gives \(\ninf{T}<1\), so Corollary 5.1 applies. Gauss–Seidel also converges under the same hypothesis; the standard argument bounds the eigenvalues of \((D-L)^{-1}U\) below \(1\) using diagonal dominance, which we state without proof. \(\square\)
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.7 Relaxation and SOR
Even when Gauss–Seidel converges, its iteration matrix may have spectral radius close to \(1\), so convergence is slow. Relaxation introduces a parameter \(\omega\ne 0\) that scales how far each step moves. Multiplying \(Ax=b\) by \(\omega\) and splitting \(\omega D=D-(1-\omega)D\) turns the system into \(\bigl(D-(1-\omega)D-\omega L-\omega U\bigr)x=\omega b\), which rearranges to \(Dx=\omega\bigl[b+(L+U)x\bigr]+(1-\omega)Dx\) and hence to the weighted Jacobi update \[
x^{(k+1)}=\omega D^{-1}\bigl[b+(L+U)x^{(k)}\bigr]+(1-\omega)x^{(k)}
=T_\omega x^{(k)}+c_\omega,
\tag{5.12}\] where \(T_\omega=(1-\omega)I+\omega D^{-1}(L+U)=I-\omega D^{-1}A\) (using \(L+U=D-A\)) and \(c_\omega=\omega D^{-1}b\). For \(\omega\in(0,1)\) this is a weighted average of a Jacobi step and “do nothing” (under-relaxation); for \(\omega>1\) it overshoots the Jacobi direction (over-relaxation); \(\omega=1\) recovers Jacobi. Grouping the lower-triangular part with the update instead — rearranging \((D-\omega L)x=\bigl[(1-\omega)D+\omega U\bigr]x+\omega b\) — gives the relaxed Gauss–Seidel iteration, successive over-relaxation (SOR): \[
x^{(k+1)}=(D-\omega L)^{-1}\bigl\{\bigl[(1-\omega)D+\omega U\bigr]x^{(k)}+\omega b\bigr\}
=T_\omega x^{(k)}+c_\omega,
\tag{5.13}\] now with \(T_\omega=(D-\omega L)^{-1}\bigl[(1-\omega)D+\omega U\bigr]\) and \(c_\omega=\omega(D-\omega L)^{-1}b\). Like every stationary iteration it converges iff \(\spec(T_\omega)<1\), and the parameter \(\omega\) is a knob to make that spectral radius small.
Two facts frame the search for a good \(\omega\). First, \(\omega\) cannot be chosen freely.

Theorem 5.7 (Kahan’s theorem) If \(a_{ii}\ne 0\) for all \(i\), then \(\spec(T_\omega)\ge\abs{\omega-1}\) for SOR. Consequently SOR can converge only if \(\omega\in(0,2)\).


Proof. The determinant of \(T_\omega\) equals the product of its eigenvalues, and a short computation with the triangular factors gives \(\det T_\omega=(1-\omega)^n\); hence \(\spec(T_\omega)^n\ge\abs{\det T_\omega}=\abs{1-\omega}^n\), so \(\spec(T_\omega)\ge\abs{1-\omega}\). This is \(<1\) only for \(\omega\in(0,2)\). (Full computation left as in the source.) \(\square\)

Second, within that window symmetry pins down convergence. Recall a symmetric matrix \(A\) is positive definite, written \(A\succ 0\), if \(x^\top Ax>0\) for all \(x\ne 0\) (see Section 5.9); such \(A\) has a unique positive definite square root \(A^{1/2}\).

Theorem 5.8 (Ostrowski–Reich theorem) If \(A\) is positive definite and \(\omega\in(0,2)\), then SOR converges for every \(b\) and every \(x^{(0)}\).


Proof. Stated without proof (see the textbook). For the special case of weighted Jacobi one shows directly that \(D^{-1}A\) is diagonalizable with positive eigenvalues when \(A\) is positive definite, so the eigenvalues \(1-\omega\lambda\) of \(T_\omega=I-\omega D^{-1}A\) lie in \((-1,1)\) precisely when \(0<\omega<2/\spec(D^{-1}A)\). \(\square\)

Choosing the optimal \(\omega\) is hard in general; a closed-form optimum is known for tridiagonal positive definite matrices (see the textbook).
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.8 Condition numbers and error sensitivity
Iterative methods stop when the residual \(r=b-A\tilde{x}\) of the current guess \(\tilde{x}\) is small. But a small residual is not the same as a small error \(\tilde{x}-x\). Consider \[
\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0.0001\end{pmatrix}\!\begin{pmatrix}x_1\\x_2\end{pmatrix}
=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix},
\qquad x=(1,0)^\top .
\] The guess \(\tilde{x}=(1,10)^\top\) has residual \(r=(0,-0.001)^\top\), tiny, yet the error is enormous. The gap between “residual small” and “error small” is measured by the condition number.

Theorem 5.9 (Residual–error bound) Let \(A\) be invertible, \(Ax=b\), and \(\tilde{x}\) a guess with residual \(r=b-A\tilde{x}\). Then in any induced norm \[
\norm{x-\tilde{x}}\le\norm{r}\,\norm{A^{-1}},
\qquad\text{and, if }x,b\ne 0,\qquad
\frac{\norm{x-\tilde{x}}}{\norm{x}}\le\norm{A}\,\norm{A^{-1}}\,\frac{\norm{r}}{\norm{b}} .
\tag{5.14}\]


Proof. Since \(x=A^{-1}b\) and \(\tilde{x}=A^{-1}(b-r)\), we get \(x-\tilde{x}=A^{-1}r\), so \(\norm{x-\tilde{x}}\le\norm{A^{-1}}\norm{r}\). For the relative bound, \(b=Ax\) gives \(\norm{b}\le\norm{A}\norm{x}\), i.e. \(1/\norm{x}\le\norm{A}/\norm{b}\); multiplying the two inequalities yields the result. \(\square\)

The amplification factor \(\norm{A}\norm{A^{-1}}\) is exactly the condition number \(\cond(A)\) introduced in 2.

Definition 5.7 (Condition number) The condition number of an invertible \(A\) relative to a norm is \(\cond(A)=\norm{A}\,\norm{A^{-1}}\). A matrix is well-conditioned if \(\cond(A)\approx 1\) and ill-conditioned if \(\cond(A)\gg 1\).


Proposition 5.2 (Condition number is at least one) \(\cond(A)\ge 1\) for every invertible \(A\).


Proof. From \(AA^{-1}=I\) and submultiplicativity, \(1=\norm{I}\le\norm{A}\,\norm{A^{-1}}=\cond(A)\). \(\square\)

For the \(2\)-norm, Equation 5.4 makes \(\cond_2(A)=\sigma_1(A)/\sigma_n(A)\) the ratio of largest to smallest singular value; for symmetric positive definite \(A\) this is the ratio of largest to smallest eigenvalue. This is the quantity that will control the speed of conjugate gradient in Section 5.12.
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.9 Positive definite systems
The rest of the chapter specializes to symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices, the setting where the sharpest iterative methods live.

Definition 5.8 (Positive (semi)definite matrix) A symmetric \(A\) is positive definite (\(A\succ 0\)) if \(x^\top Ax>0\) for all \(x\ne 0\), and positive semidefinite (\(A\succeq 0\)) if \(x^\top Ax\ge 0\) for all \(x\).

The following characterizations are used repeatedly; each is standard linear algebra (Chapter 7 develops the eigenvalue side).

Proposition 5.3 (Characterizations of positive definiteness) For symmetric \(A\) the following are equivalent: (i) \(A\succ 0\); (ii) \(A=B^\top B\) for some invertible \(B\); (iii) \(A\) has strictly positive eigenvalues; (iv) \(A\) has a unique positive definite square root \(A^{1/2}\). When \(A\succ 0\), the spectral theorem gives \(A=U\Lambda U^\top\) with \(U\) orthogonal and \(\Lambda\succ 0\) diagonal, and \(A^{1/2}=U\Lambda^{1/2}U^\top\).

The key structural fact is that solving an SPD system is the same problem as minimizing a quadratic. This is what opens the door to optimization methods.

Theorem 5.10 (SPD solve as minimization) Let \(A\succ 0\). The unique solution \(x\) of \(Ax=b\) is the unique minimizer of the quadratic \[
g(x)=\tfrac12\,x^\top Ax-b^\top x .
\tag{5.15}\]


Proof. Using \(\nabla(x^\top Cx)=2Cx\) for symmetric \(C\) and \(\nabla(a^\top x)=a\), the gradient is \[
\nabla g(x)=Ax-b,
\] so \(\nabla g(x)=0\) iff \(Ax=b\): the unique critical point is the solution. The Hessian is \(\nabla^2 g\equiv A\succ 0\), so \(g\) is strictly convex and its only critical point is the global minimizer. \(\square\)







TipAny linear system reduces to an SPD one



For a general invertible \(B\), the least-squares reformulation \(\min_x\ntwo{Bx-c}^2\) has gradient condition \(B^\top Bx=B^\top c\), the normal equations, whose matrix \(B^\top B\succ 0\). So “once you can solve SPD systems, you can solve every system.” For a square invertible \(B\) solving \(Bx=c\) directly is usually better, but for rectangular \(B\in\R^{m\times n}\) with full column rank the normal equations are the natural route (revisited in Chapter 6).


The graph of \(g\) is a bowl whose level sets are ellipsoids; their eccentricity is set by \(\cond_2(A)\). A well-conditioned \(A\) gives nearly circular level sets, an ill-conditioned \(A\) gives long thin valleys — the geometry that makes naive descent struggle, as we see next.
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.10 Steepest descent
To minimize \(g\) we can walk downhill. Given the current guess \(x^{(k-1)}\), move along the direction of steepest descent and pick the step length optimally, \[
x^{(k)}=x^{(k-1)}+t^{(k)}v^{(k)} .
\] Multivariable calculus says the steepest-descent direction is the negative gradient, which here is exactly the residual, \[
v^{(k)}=-\nabla g(x^{(k-1)})=b-Ax^{(k-1)}=:r^{(k-1)} .
\tag{5.16}\] Given a search direction \(v\), the optimal step solves the one-variable problem \(\min_t g(x^{(k-1)}+t v)\). Writing \(x=x^{(k-1)}\), \(r=r^{(k-1)}\), \(v=v^{(k)}\) and expanding, \[
h(t)=g(x+tv)=\tfrac{t^2}{2}v^\top Av+t\,(v^\top Ax)-t\,(v^\top b)+g(x),
\] so \(h'(t)=0\) gives the exact line-search step \[
t^{(k)}=\frac{v^\top(b-Ax)}{v^\top Av}
=\frac{\inner{v^{(k)}}{r^{(k-1)}}}{\inner{v^{(k)}}{Av^{(k)}}} .
\tag{5.17}\] This formula for \(t^{(k)}\) holds for any direction \(v^{(k)}\), a fact the conjugate gradient method will reuse; for steepest descent specifically \(v^{(k)}=r^{(k-1)}\).






NoteAlgorithm — Steepest descent



Given \(x^{(0)}\), \(b\), matvecs by \(A\succ 0\), a cap \(m\), and a relative tolerance \(\eps\): set \(r^{(0)}=b-Ax^{(0)}\); for \(k=1,2,\dots,m\):

\(v^{(k)}=r^{(k-1)}\);
\(t^{(k)}=\dfrac{\inner{v^{(k)}}{r^{(k-1)}}}{\inner{v^{(k)}}{Av^{(k)}}}\);
\(x^{(k)}=x^{(k-1)}+t^{(k)}v^{(k)}\);
\(r^{(k)}=b-Ax^{(k)}\);
if \(\ntwo{r^{(k)}}/\ntwo{b}\le\eps\), stop.



Steepest descent makes the same mistake repeatedly: on an ill-conditioned bowl it zig-zags across the narrow valley, each step partly undoing the last. Its residual directions ignore the history of directions already explored. Fixing this is the idea of conjugate gradient.
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.11 The conjugate gradient method
Imagine first \(A=I\): the level sets are spheres and steepest descent points straight at the minimum. More generally, if we could make our search directions orthogonal we would finish in \(n\) steps. The change of variables \(y=Bx\) with \(B^\top B=A\) (for instance \(B=A^{1/2}\)) turns \(g\) into \(\tilde g(y)=\tfrac12 y^\top y-\tilde b^\top y\), the identity-matrix problem — so the directions \(v^{(k)}\) should be orthogonal after transformation, i.e. \((Bv^{(k)})^\top(Bv^{(l)})=0\). Since \((Bv^{(k)})^\top(Bv^{(l)})=v^{(k)\top}A v^{(l)}\), the right notion is orthogonality in the \(A\)-inner product.

Definition 5.9 (A-orthogonality) For \(A\succ 0\) the \(A\)-inner product is \(\inner{u}{v}_A:=u^\top Av\). Vectors \(v^{(1)},\dots,v^{(m)}\) are \(A\)-orthogonal (conjugate) if \(\inner{v^{(k)}}{v^{(l)}}_A=0\) for all \(k\ne l\).

Conjugate gradient (CG) builds such directions on the fly. At step \(k\) it takes the steepest-descent starter \(r^{(k-1)}\) and \(A\)-orthogonalizes it against the previous directions by Gram–Schmidt in the \(A\)-inner product: \[
v^{(k)}=r^{(k-1)}-\sum_{j=1}^{k-1}
\frac{\inner{r^{(k-1)}}{v^{(j)}}_A}{\inner{v^{(j)}}{v^{(j)}}_A}\,v^{(j)} .
\] The remarkable fact — the engine of CG’s efficiency — is that in this construction the new residual \(r^{(k-1)}\) is already \(A\)-orthogonal to every earlier direction except the immediately preceding one, so the long sum collapses to a single term. The full orthogonalization then costs nothing beyond a short two-term recurrence, \[
v^{(1)}=r^{(0)},\qquad
v^{(k)}=r^{(k-1)}+s^{(k-1)}v^{(k-1)},\qquad
s^{(k-1)}=-\frac{\inner{r^{(k-1)}}{Av^{(k-1)}}}{\inner{v^{(k-1)}}{Av^{(k-1)}}} .
\tag{5.18}\] The step length is the same exact line search Equation 5.17. One more trick keeps the cost at a single matrix–vector product (“matvec”) per iteration: never recompute \(Ax^{(k)}\) from scratch, but carry \(w^{(k)}:=Ax^{(k)}\) forward via \(w^{(k)}=w^{(k-1)}+t^{(k)}u^{(k)}\) where \(u^{(k)}:=Av^{(k)}\) is the one matvec of the step. Then \(r^{(k)}=b-w^{(k)}\) needs no new matvec.






NoteAlgorithm — Conjugate gradient (one matvec per step)



Given \(x^{(0)}\), \(b\), matvecs by \(A\succ 0\), a cap \(m\), and tolerance \(\eps\): compute \(w^{(0)}=Ax^{(0)}\), set \(r^{(0)}=b-w^{(0)}\). For \(k=1,2,\dots,m\):

if \(k=1\), \(v^{(1)}=r^{(0)}\); else \(v^{(k)}=r^{(k-1)}+s^{(k-1)}v^{(k-1)}\);
\(u^{(k)}=Av^{(k)}\) (the only matvec);
\(t^{(k)}=\dfrac{\inner{v^{(k)}}{r^{(k-1)}}}{\inner{v^{(k)}}{u^{(k)}}}\);
\(x^{(k)}=x^{(k-1)}+t^{(k)}v^{(k)}\) and \(w^{(k)}=w^{(k-1)}+t^{(k)}u^{(k)}\);
\(r^{(k)}=b-w^{(k)}\); if \(\ntwo{r^{(k)}}/\ntwo{b}\le\eps\), stop;
\(s^{(k)}=-\dfrac{\inner{r^{(k)}}{u^{(k)}}}{\inner{v^{(k)}}{u^{(k)}}}\).



The cost per iteration is therefore one matvec — the same as steepest descent — but the directions now account for the whole history, which is what buys the dramatically faster convergence of Figure 5.1. A minimal implementation of the loop:
import numpy as np

def conjugate_gradient(A, b, x0, m, eps):
    x = x0.copy()
    r = b - A @ x                    # residual
    nb = np.linalg.norm(b)
    v = r.copy()
    for k in range(m):
        if k > 0:
            v = r + s * v            # A-orthogonalize (two-term recurrence)
        u = A @ v                    # the one matvec
        t = (v @ r) / (v @ u)        # exact line search
        x = x + t * v
        r = r - t * u                # r = b - A x, updated without a new matvec
        if np.linalg.norm(r) / nb <= eps:
            break
        s = -(r @ u) / (v @ u)
    return x
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.12 Convergence of CG
Because the \(n\) directions CG produces are \(A\)-orthogonal, they form a basis and CG reaches the exact minimizer once it has searched all \(n\) of them.

Theorem 5.11 (Finite termination of CG) Applied to \(Ax=b\) with \(A\succ 0\), conjugate gradient recovers the exact solution in at most \(n\) iterations: \(x^{(k)}=x\) for all \(k\ge n\) (in exact arithmetic).


Proof. The search directions \(v^{(1)},\dots,v^{(n)}\) are \(A\)-orthogonal, hence linearly independent, hence a basis of \(\R^n\). Exact line search along each makes the error \(A\)-orthogonal to that direction, so after \(n\) steps the error is \(A\)-orthogonal to a full basis and must be zero. (Handwavy version of the source’s picture-proof.) \(\square\)

Finite termination alone already gives an \(\bigO(n^2)\) solver when each matvec costs \(\bigO(n)\) (as for sparse \(A\)), beating the \(\bigO(n^3)\) of a direct factorization. But the real power of CG is that it usually converges to good accuracy in far fewer than \(n\) iterations, at a rate set by the condition number.

Theorem 5.12 (Convergence rate of conjugate gradient) If \(A\succ 0\) has \(2\)-norm condition number \(\cond(A)=\cond_2(A)\), then the CG iterates for \(Ax=b\) satisfy \[
\ntwo{x^{(k)}-x}\le 2\,\cond(A)\left(\frac{\sqrt{\cond(A)}-1}{\sqrt{\cond(A)}+1}\right)^{k}
\ntwo{x^{(0)}-x} .
\tag{5.19}\]


Proof. Stated without proof (the standard argument bounds the \(A\)-norm of the error by a Chebyshev polynomial on the spectrum of \(A\)). For SPD \(A\), \(\cond_2(A)\) is the ratio of largest to smallest eigenvalue. The important reading is the base: when \(\cond(A)\) is large, \[
\frac{\sqrt{\cond(A)}-1}{\sqrt{\cond(A)}+1}\approx 1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{\cond(A)}},
\] so CG’s error decays geometrically at a rate governed by \(\sqrt{\cond(A)}\). \(\square\)

The contrast with the stationary methods is the whole point: Jacobi and Gauss–Seidel decay with base roughly \(1-2/\cond(A)\), whereas CG decays with base roughly \(1-2/\sqrt{\cond(A)}\) — a square-root improvement in the condition number that translates into far fewer iterations on ill-conditioned problems. Figure 5.1 shows the two descent methods on the same SPD system: steepest descent crawls while CG plunges.






Figure 5.1: Relative residual \(\ntwo{r^{(k)}}/\ntwo{b}\) versus iteration for a random SPD system of size \(n=100\) with \(\cond_2(A)=10^3\). Steepest descent zig-zags down the ill-conditioned bowl and stalls; conjugate gradient, reusing the history of search directions, drives the residual down many orders of magnitude in a fraction of the iterations.
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.13 Preconditioning
The rate Equation 5.19 says everything hinges on \(\cond(A)\): shrink it and CG converges faster. Preconditioning does exactly that by solving an equivalent, better-conditioned system. Suppose we can find \(C\) with \(A\approx CC^\top\) and with \(C\), \(C^\top\) easy to invert. Then \[
\tilde A:=C^{-1}AC^{-\top}\approx I
\] is well-conditioned and still SPD, and \(A=C\tilde A C^\top\) gives the equivalent system \[
\tilde A\,\tilde x=\tilde b,\qquad \tilde x=C^\top x,\quad \tilde b=C^{-1}b .
\tag{5.20}\] Preconditioned CG applies CG to Equation 5.20, then recovers \(x=C^{-\top}\tilde x\); each matvec by \(\tilde A\) is realized as a matvec by \(A\) sandwiched between solves with \(C^{-1}\) and \(C^{-\top}\). Finding \(C\) with \(C^{-1}AC^{-\top}\approx I\) and cheap solves is hard in general, and it is the central art of practical iterative solving. One cheap default is the diagonal (Jacobi) preconditioner \[
C=D^{1/2},
\] with \(D\) the diagonal of \(A\): then \(C^{-1}=C^{-\top}\) is a diagonal matrix with entries \(1/\sqrt{a_{ii}}\), and the preconditioned matrix \(\tilde A=(\tilde a_{ij})\) has \(\tilde a_{ii}=1\) for all \(i\) — a simple rescaling that already helps when \(A\)’s diagonal varies widely.
crumbs: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems

title: 5  Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
text:
5.14 Chapter summary

Vector norms (Definition 5.1) and induced matrix norms (Definition 5.3) measure convergence; on \(\R^n\) all norms are equivalent (Theorem 5.2), and useful closed forms are \(\ninf{A}=\max_i\sum_j\abs{a_{ij}}\) (Theorem 5.3) and \(\ntwo{A}=\sigma_1(A)\) (Equation 5.4).
The spectral radius \(\spec(A)\) (Definition 5.4) satisfies \(\spec(A)\le\norm{A}\) and controls powers of \(A\): \(A\) is convergent iff \(\spec(A)<1\) (Theorem 5.4).
Jacobi (Equation 5.6) and Gauss–Seidel (Equation 5.8) split \(A=D-L-U\) and iterate \(x^{(k+1)}=Tx^{(k)}+c\); such an iteration converges for all data iff \(\spec(T)<1\) (Theorem 5.5), which holds whenever \(A\) is strictly diagonally dominant (Theorem 5.6), with rate \(\norm{T}^k\) (Corollary 5.1).
SOR (Equation 5.13) relaxes Gauss–Seidel by \(\omega\); convergence requires \(\omega\in(0,2)\)

and is guaranteed there when \(A\succ 0\) (Theorem 5.8).

A small residual need not mean a small error; the gap is the condition number \(\cond(A)=\norm{A}\norm{A^{-1}}\ge 1\) (Definition 5.7, Theorem 5.9), matching



For \(A\succ 0\), solving \(Ax=b\) equals minimizing \(g(x)=\tfrac12x^\top Ax-b^\top x\) (Theorem 5.10). Steepest descent follows the residual with exact line search Equation 5.17 but zig-zags on ill-conditioned bowls.
Conjugate gradient builds \(A\)-orthogonal directions by a two-term recurrence

at one matvec per step, terminates exactly in \(\le n\) steps (Theorem 5.11), and converges at rate \(\bigl(\tfrac{\sqrt{\cond}-1}{\sqrt{\cond}+1}\bigr)^k\) (Theorem 5.12) — a square-root improvement over the stationary methods (Figure 5.1).

Preconditioning replaces \(A\) by \(\tilde A=C^{-1}AC^{-\top}\approx I\) to shrink the condition number; the diagonal choice \(C=D^{1/2}\) is the cheap default.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.1 Least-squares approximation
Interpolation (Chapter 1) insists that the approximant pass through the data. That is the wrong demand when the data are noisy, when there are far more samples than we want parameters, or when we care about the average error over a whole interval rather than the error at a handful of points. Approximation theory relaxes exactness to best fit: choose the member of a restricted class of functions that is closest to \(f\) in a chosen norm. Almost everything in this chapter flows from one idea — best approximation in an inner-product norm is orthogonal projection, and the computation collapses to a linear system that becomes trivial once the basis is orthogonal. That observation organizes least squares, orthogonal polynomials, Chebyshev economization, Gauss quadrature, and — through the complex exponentials — Fourier series, the DFT, and the FFT.
Suppose we are given data \((x_i,y_i)\), \(i=1,\dots,m\), with \(x_i,y_i\in\R\), and we want a function \(f\) with \(f(x_i)\approx y_i\) drawn from a restricted class (to avoid overfitting). The tractable case is a class spanned by fixed basis functions \(g_0,\dots,g_{p-1}\), \[
f_{\mathbf a}(x)=\sum_{j=0}^{p-1} a_j\,g_j(x),
\tag{6.1}\] parametrized linearly by \(\mathbf a=(a_0,\dots,a_{p-1})\in\R^{p}\) (we zero-index the components). The choice \(g_j(x)=x^{j}\) recovers polynomial least squares; \(g_1(x)=x,\,g_0(x)=1\) recovers straight-line fitting. In least squares we minimize the sum of squared residuals \[
\min_{\mathbf a\in\R^{p}} F(\mathbf a),\qquad F(\mathbf a)\defeq\sum_{i=1}^{m}\bigl(f_{\mathbf a}(x_i)-y_i\bigr)^2 .
\] Collecting the basis values into the \(m\times p\) matrix \(G=(g_{ij})\) with \(g_{ij}=g_j(x_i)\) (rows indexed \(i=1,\dots,m\), columns \(j=0,\dots,p-1\)), the objective is exactly a squared Euclidean norm, \[
\sum_{i=1}^{m}\Bigl(\textstyle\sum_{j=0}^{p-1} g_{ij}a_j-y_i\Bigr)^2
=\ntwo{G\mathbf a-y}^2 ,
\] so least squares is the linear-algebra problem \(\min_{\mathbf a}\ntwo{G\mathbf a-y}^2\). We solved exactly this in Chapter 4; the solution is the normal equations.
Some nonlinear classes reduce to Equation 6.1 after a transformation. To fit the exponential model \(f_{b,c}(x)=b\,e^{cx}\) we take logarithms, \(\log y_i\approx \log b+c\,x_i\), and run linear least squares on the transformed data \(\tilde y_i=\log y_i\) against \(x_i\), recovering \(b=e^{a_0}\), \(c=a_1\). This is not identical to nonlinear least squares on the original residuals — it minimizes error in \(\log y\), not in \(y\) — but it avoids a general optimization.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
Theorem 6.1 (Least squares and the normal equations) Let \(G\in\R^{m\times p}\) with \(g_{ij}=g_j(x_i)\) and \(y\in\R^{m}\). Every minimizer of \(\ntwo{G\mathbf a-y}^2\) satisfies the normal equations \[
G^\top G\,\mathbf a=G^\top y .
\tag{6.2}\] The matrix \(G^\top G\) is symmetric positive semidefinite; it is positive definite — so the minimizer is unique — if and only if \(G\) has rank \(p\), which requires \(m\ge p\). For polynomial least squares (\(g_j(x)=x^{j}\)), distinctness of the nodes \(x_1,\dots,x_m\) already forces \(\rank G=p\).


Proof. \(F(\mathbf a)=\ntwo{G\mathbf a-y}^2\) is a smooth convex quadratic with gradient \(\nabla F(\mathbf a)=2G^\top(G\mathbf a-y)\). Setting it to zero gives Equation 6.2. For any \(v\in\R^{p}\), \(v^\top G^\top G\,v=\ntwo{Gv}^2\ge0\), with equality iff \(Gv=0\); thus \(G^\top G\succ0\) exactly when \(G\) has trivial null space, i.e. rank \(p\). For \(g_j(x)=x^j\) the matrix \(G\) is a Vandermonde matrix, whose columns are independent when the nodes are distinct. \(\square\)

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.2 Interlude: inner products and Gram–Schmidt
To move from discrete data to functions on a whole interval we need the geometry of inner-product spaces, recalled here for continuous functions.

Definition 6.1 (Inner product and the \(L^2\) inner products) An inner product \(\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}\) on a real vector space \(\mathcal V\) is a map \(\mathcal V\times\mathcal V\to\R\) that is linear and homogeneous in the first slot (\(\inner{u+v}{w}=\inner{u}{w}+\inner{v}{w}\), \(\inner{\lambda u}{v}=\lambda\inner{u}{v}\)), symmetric (\(\inner{u}{v}=\inner{v}{u}\)), and positive definite (\(\inner{u}{u}\ge0\) with equality iff \(u=0\)). It induces the norm \(\norm{u}=\sqrt{\inner{u}{u}}\) and the notion of orthogonality, \(u\perp v\iff\inner{u}{v}=0\). On \(C[a,b]\) the \(L^2\) inner product and its weighted version with a nonnegative weight \(w:(a,b)\to[0,\infty)\) are \[
\inner{f}{g}_{L^2([a,b])}=\int_a^b f(x)g(x)\,\dd x,\qquad
\inner{f}{g}_{L^2([a,b];w)}=\int_a^b f(x)g(x)\,w(x)\,\dd x .
\]

The dot product on \(\R^n\) is the archetype; the \(A\)-inner product \(\inner{x}{y}_A=x^\top A y\) of conjugate gradient (Chapter 5) is another. The space \(C[a,b]\) is a vector space under pointwise operations, and the polynomials of degree \(\le n\) form a subspace \(\mathbb P_n\) with the monomials \(M_k(x)=x^{k}\), \(k=0,\dots,n\), as a basis.
Given an orthogonal collection \(v_1,\dots,v_k\) and a new vector \(w\), the Gram–Schmidt procedure appends \[
v_{k+1}=w-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{\inner{w}{v_i}}{\inner{v_i}{v_i}}\,v_i ,
\tag{6.3}\] which is orthogonal to every \(v_i\) (check: \(\inner{v_{k+1}}{v_i}=\inner{w}{v_i}-\inner{w}{v_i}=0\)) and preserves the span. This one formula, applied to the monomials under a weighted \(L^2\) inner product, manufactures the orthogonal polynomials of Section 6.4.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.3 Least squares over an interval
Now approximate a function \(f\) on an entire interval \([a,b]\) by \(f_{\mathbf a}\) of the form Equation 6.1 — “infinitely many data points” \((x,f(x))\). Discretize with the uniform grid \(x_i=a+ih\), \(h=(b-a)/m\), \(y_i=f(x_i)\). Up to the fixed factor \(h=(b-a)/m\) (which does not move the minimizer) the discrete least-squares objective is a Riemann sum, \[
h\sum_{i=1}^{m}\bigl(f_{\mathbf a}(x_i)-f(x_i)\bigr)^2
\;\xrightarrow[m\to\infty]{}\;
\int_a^b\bigl(f_{\mathbf a}(x)-f(x)\bigr)^2\,\dd x=\norm{f_{\mathbf a}-f}_{L^2([a,b])}^2 .
\] Take the same limit in the normal equations. Multiply Equation 6.2 by \(h\) and pass \(m\to\infty\): each entry is again a Riemann sum, \[
[hG^\top G]_{ij}=\sum_{k=1}^{m} g_i(x_k)g_j(x_k)\,h\to\int_a^b g_i(x)g_j(x)\,\dd x=\inner{g_i}{g_j}_{L^2([a,b])},
\] \[
[hG^\top y]_{i}=\sum_{k=1}^{m} g_i(x_k)f(x_k)\,h\to\int_a^b g_i(x)f(x)\,\dd x=\inner{g_i}{f}_{L^2([a,b])} .
\] So continuous least squares is solved by the Gram matrix system \(A\mathbf a=b\) with \[
A_{ij}=\inner{g_i}{g_j}_{L^2([a,b])},\qquad b_i=\inner{g_i}{f}_{L^2([a,b])},
\tag{6.4}\] after which \(f_{\mathbf a}=\sum_j a_j g_j\). The weighted problem \(\min_{\mathbf a}\norm{f_{\mathbf a}-f}_{L^2([a,b];w)}^2\) is identical with every inner product replaced by its \(w\)-weighted version; \(w\equiv1\) recovers the unweighted case. When the entries cannot be integrated in closed form they are estimated by numerical quadrature — a hook we return to in Section 6.8.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.4 Orthogonal polynomials
The Gram matrix Equation 6.4 is generally dense, and for the monomial basis it is the notoriously ill-conditioned Hilbert-type matrix. The remedy is to choose a basis that makes \(A\) diagonal: an orthogonal basis. Writing \(\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}_w\) for \(\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}_{L^2([a,b];w)}\):

Definition 6.2 (Orthogonal polynomials) A sequence of polynomials \(p_0,p_1,\dots\) with \(\deg p_k=k\) is an orthogonal polynomial sequence with respect to the weight \(w\) on \([a,b]\) if \[
\inner{p_i}{p_j}_w=0\qquad\text{whenever } i\ne j .
\] The sequence is orthonormal if in addition \(\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w=1\) for all \(k\); it is monic if each \(p_k\) has leading coefficient \(1\), i.e. \(p_k(x)=x^{k}+\cdots\). (The Lindsey notes and this book write the family as \(p_0,\dots,p_n\); the standard textbook symbol is \(\phi_0,\dots,\phi_n\).)

Gram–Schmidt on the monomials builds the monic sequence explicitly.

Proposition 6.1 (Gram–Schmidt construction) Let \(M_k(x)=x^{k}\). Set \(p_0=M_0=1\) and inductively \[
p_{k+1}=M_{k+1}-\sum_{i=0}^{k}\frac{\inner{M_{k+1}}{p_i}_w}{\inner{p_i}{p_i}_w}\,p_i .
\tag{6.5}\] Then each \(p_k\) is monic of degree \(k\), the sequence is orthogonal with respect to \(\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}_w\), and \(\Span(p_0,\dots,p_k)=\Span(M_0,\dots,M_k)=\mathbb P_k\). Consequently, for \(k>\ell\) the polynomial \(p_k\) is orthogonal to every element of \(\mathbb P_\ell\).


Proof. Formula Equation 6.5 is Equation 6.3 applied to \(w=M_{k+1}\) against \(p_0,\dots,p_k\), so \(p_{k+1}\perp p_i\) for \(i\le k\); by induction the whole sequence is orthogonal. Subtracting a combination of lower-degree polynomials leaves the leading \(x^{k+1}\) untouched, so \(p_{k+1}\) is monic of degree \(k+1\). Gram–Schmidt preserves spans, and \(\Span(M_0,\dots,M_k)=\mathbb P_k\), giving the span claim; orthogonal (hence independent) polynomials of degrees \(0,\dots,k\) form a basis of \(\mathbb P_k\). Finally, if \(k>\ell\) then \(p_k\perp p_0,\dots,p_\ell\), and these span \(\mathbb P_\ell\), so \(p_k\perp\mathbb P_\ell\). \(\square\)

The payoff for least squares is immediate: with \(g_i=p_i\) the Gram matrix is diagonal, \(A_{ij}=\inner{p_i}{p_i}_w\,\delta_{ij}\), so the normal equations decouple.

Theorem 6.2 (Least-squares projection onto \(\mathbb P_n\)) For the weight \(w\) on \([a,b]\), the problem \(\displaystyle\min_{p\in\mathbb P_n}\norm{p-f}_w^2\) is solved by \[
p=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{\inner{f}{p_k}_w}{\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w}\,p_k ,
\tag{6.6}\] where \(\{p_k\}\) is any orthogonal polynomial sequence for \(w\) on \([a,b]\).


Proof. Since \(\{f_{\mathbf a}:\mathbf a\in\R^{n+1}\}=\Span(p_0,\dots,p_n)=\mathbb P_n\), minimizing over \(\mathbb P_n\) is the interval least-squares problem in the basis \(\{p_k\}\). Its Gram matrix is diagonal, so the \(i\)-th normal equation is \(\inner{p_i}{p_i}_w\,a_i=\inner{f}{p_i}_w\), giving \(a_i=\inner{f}{p_i}_w/\inner{p_i}{p_i}_w\) and Equation 6.6. \(\square\)

Formula Equation 6.6 is orthogonal projection: \(p\) is the truncated generalized Fourier series of \(f\), and \(f-p\perp\mathbb P_n\).

The three-term recurrence
Running Equation 6.5 literally requires \(k+1\) inner products at step \(k\). Almost all of them vanish: orthogonal polynomials obey a three-term recurrence, the computational workhorse of the subject.

Theorem 6.3 (Three-term recurrence) Let \(\{p_k\}\) be the monic orthogonal polynomial sequence for \(w\) on \([a,b]\). Then \[
p_{k+1}(x)=(x-\alpha_k)\,p_k(x)-\beta_k\,p_{k-1}(x),\qquad k\ge1,
\tag{6.7}\] with \(p_0=1\), \(p_1(x)=x-\alpha_0\), and \[
\alpha_k=\frac{\inner{xp_k}{p_k}_w}{\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w},\qquad
\beta_k=\frac{\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w}{\inner{p_{k-1}}{p_{k-1}}_w}>0 .
\]


Proof. The Lindsey notes state this without proof; the standard argument is short. Because \(xp_k\) is monic of degree \(k+1\), the difference \(xp_k-p_{k+1}\) lies in \(\mathbb P_k=\Span(p_0,\dots,p_k)\), so \(xp_k=p_{k+1}+\sum_{j=0}^{k}c_j p_j\) for some coefficients. Taking \(\inner{\cdot}{p_i}_w\) and using orthogonality gives \(c_i=\inner{xp_k}{p_i}_w/\inner{p_i}{p_i}_w\). Now \(\inner{xp_k}{p_i}_w=
\inner{p_k}{xp_i}_w\), and for \(i\le k-2\) the polynomial \(xp_i\) has degree \(\le k-1\), hence is orthogonal to \(p_k\) by 1; so \(c_i=0\) for \(i\le k-2\). Only \(c_k=\alpha_k\) and \(c_{k-1}\) survive. For the last, \(xp_{k-1}=p_k+(\text{lower degree})\), so \(\inner{xp_k}{p_{k-1}}_w=\inner{p_k}{xp_{k-1}}_w=\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w\), whence \(c_{k-1}=\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w/\inner{p_{k-1}}{p_{k-1}}_w=\beta_k>0\). Rearranging \(xp_k=p_{k+1}+\alpha_k p_k+\beta_k p_{k-1}\) yields Equation 6.7. \(\square\)







ImportantEvaluate by the recurrence, never by the coefficients



One could expand the recurrence into \(p_n(x)=a_nx^n+\dots+a_0\) and evaluate that. Do not: the coefficients \(a_0,\dots,a_n\) grow so fast that they overflow for moderate \(n\). The numerically stable evaluation builds \(p_0(x),\dots,p_n(x)\) from scratch by Equation 6.7, at \(\bigO(n)\) cost, given the recurrence coefficients \(\alpha_k,\beta_k\).


Legendre polynomials. For \(w\equiv1\) on \([-1,1]\) the monic orthogonal polynomials are the (monic) Legendre polynomials \(P_k\), satisfying \[
P_{k+1}(x)=x\,P_k(x)-\frac{k^{2}}{4k^{2}-1}\,P_{k-1}(x),\qquad k\ge1,
\] with \(P_0=1\), \(P_1(x)=x\). The recurrence generates \[
P_2=x^2-\tfrac13,\qquad P_3=x^3-\tfrac35x,\qquad P_4=x^4-\tfrac67x^2+\tfrac{3}{35},
\] where e.g. \(P_2=xP_1-\tfrac13P_0=x^2-\tfrac13\) using \(\alpha_1=0\), \(\beta_1=\tfrac13\). (These are monic; the classical Legendre polynomials carry a different normalization.)

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.5 Chebyshev polynomials
The single most useful orthogonal family in numerical analysis is the Chebyshev polynomials, prized for their equioscillation on \([-1,1]\). We define them trigonometrically and only afterward discover that they are orthogonal polynomials.

Definition 6.3 (Chebyshev polynomials) For \(x\in[-1,1]\) the \(n\)-th Chebyshev polynomial is \[
T_n(x)=\cos\!\bigl(n\arccos x\bigr).
\tag{6.8}\]

It is not obvious that Equation 6.8 is a polynomial. That follows from the three-term recurrence \[
T_{n+1}(x)=2x\,T_n(x)-T_{n-1}(x),\qquad T_0\equiv1,\ T_1(x)=x .
\tag{6.9}\]

Proof. Write \(\theta=\arccos x\), so \(x=\cos\theta\). The identity \(\cos(a+b)+\cos(a-b)=2\cos a\cos b\) with \(a=n\theta\), \(b=\theta\) gives \[
T_{n+1}(x)=\cos((n+1)\theta)=2\cos\theta\cos(n\theta)-\cos((n-1)\theta)=2x\,T_n(x)-T_{n-1}(x).
\] Starting from the polynomials \(T_0=1\), \(T_1=x\), the recurrence produces a polynomial at every step. \(\square\)

From Equation 6.8, \(\abs{T_n(x)}\le1\) on \([-1,1]\), and the structure of \(\cos\) pins down the zeros and extrema exactly. The zeros — the Chebyshev nodes — are \[
x_j=\cos\!\Bigl(\frac{2j-1}{2n}\pi\Bigr),\qquad j=1,\dots,n,
\tag{6.10}\] solving \(n\arccos x=(j-\tfrac12)\pi\). The extrema, where \(n\arccos x=j\pi\), are at \(x=\cos(j\pi/n)\), \(j=0,\dots,n\), and there \(T_n=\pm1\) with alternating sign. This alternation of \(+1\) and \(-1\) across the interval (Figure 6.1) is the equioscillation property.






Figure 6.1: Chebyshev polynomials \(T_0,\dots,T_4\) on \([-1,1]\). Each \(T_n\) with \(n\ge1\) oscillates between \(-1\) and \(+1\), touching the extreme values \(n+1\) times with alternating sign (\(T_0\equiv1\) is the flat exception).



That they are orthogonal polynomials comes from a change of variables.

Proposition 6.2 (Chebyshev orthogonality) The \(T_n\) are orthogonal on \([-1,1]\) with respect to the weight \(w(x)=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\).


Proof. First, \(f_n(y)=\cos(ny)\) are orthogonal on \([0,\pi]\) under the unweighted \(L^2\) inner product: for \(m\ne n\), using \(\cos(my)\cos(ny)=\tfrac12[\cos((m+n)y)+\cos((m-n)y)]\), \[
\int_0^{\pi}\cos(my)\cos(ny)\,\dd y
=\tfrac12\!\int_0^{\pi}\!\cos((m{+}n)y)\,\dd y+\tfrac12\!\int_0^{\pi}\!\cos((m{-}n)y)\,\dd y=0,
\] since \(\sin(k\pi)=0\) for every integer \(k\). Substituting \(y=\arccos x\) (so \(\dd y=-\dd x/\sqrt{1-x^2}\)) turns this into \[
0=\int_{-1}^{1}T_m(x)T_n(x)\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,\dd x=\inner{T_m}{T_n}_{L^2([-1,1];w)} .\qquad\square
\]

The recurrence Equation 6.9 doubles the leading coefficient at each step, so \(T_n\) has leading term \(2^{\,n-1}x^{n}\) for \(n\ge1\) and is not monic. The monic Chebyshev polynomial is \[
\tilde T_n(x)=\frac{1}{2^{\,n-1}}\,T_n(x)\quad(n\ge1),\qquad \tilde T_0=1,
\tag{6.11}\] so \(\max_{[-1,1]}\abs{\tilde T_n}=2^{-(n-1)}\). To work on a general \([a,b]\), transport everything through the affine map \(\tilde x=\tfrac12[(b-a)x+a+b]\), which sends \([-1,1]\) to \([a,b]\); the nodes, weight, and polynomials carry over unchanged in form.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.6 Economization of power series
Among all monic degree-\(n\) polynomials, the monic Chebyshev polynomial is the flattest on \([-1,1]\) — it has the smallest possible maximum modulus. This minimax property is what makes Chebyshev polynomials the right tool for shaving the degree off a truncated series.

Theorem 6.4 (Chebyshev minimax property) Let \(\tilde{\mathbb P}_n\) be the monic polynomials of degree \(n\). For every \(\tilde p_n\in\tilde{\mathbb P}_n\), \[
\frac{1}{2^{\,n-1}}=\max_{x\in[-1,1]}\abs{\tilde T_n(x)}\ \le\ \max_{x\in[-1,1]}\abs{\tilde p_n(x)} .
\]


Proof. The Lindsey notes cite the textbook for the full proof; the standard one-line reason is an equioscillation argument. If some monic \(\tilde p_n\) had strictly smaller maximum modulus than \(\tilde T_n\), then \(\tilde T_n-\tilde p_n\in\mathbb P_{n-1}\) would inherit the sign of \(\tilde T_n\) at each of its \(n+1\) alternating extrema, hence change sign \(n\) times and have \(n\) zeros — impossible for a nonzero polynomial of degree \(\le n-1\). So no such \(\tilde p_n\) exists. \(\square\)

Now suppose we hold a polynomial \(p_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^n a_k x^k\) (perhaps a Taylor polynomial truncated at high order) whose degree \(n\) is larger than we want, and we seek a lower-degree \(p_m\in\mathbb P_m\), \(m<n\), that stays close on \([-1,1]\). Do it greedily, dropping one degree at a time; it suffices to understand the first step, choosing \(p_{n-1}\in\mathbb P_{n-1}\) to minimize \(\max_{[-1,1]}\abs{p_n-p_{n-1}}\). Assuming \(a_n\ne0\), the polynomial \((p_n-p_{n-1})/a_n\) is monic of degree \(n\), so minimizing the maximum is equivalent to minimizing \(\abs{a_n}\max_{[-1,1]}\abs{(p_n-p_{n-1})/a_n}\), which by Theorem 6.4 is smallest exactly when \((p_n-p_{n-1})/a_n=\tilde T_n\). Solving, \[
p_{n-1}(x)=p_n(x)-a_n\,\tilde T_n(x)
\tag{6.12}\] — subtract off the leading coefficient times the monic Chebyshev polynomial. This is economization; Equation 6.12 even works when \(a_n=0\) (it does nothing), so no case analysis is needed. Iterating Equation 6.12 from degree \(n\) down to \(m\) yields a low-degree approximation whose worst-case error on \([-1,1]\) is controlled by the discarded \(\abs{a_k}/2^{k-1}\).

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.7 Lagrange interpolation meets Chebyshev polynomials
The same minimax fact answers a question left open in Chapter 1: where should we place interpolation nodes? Recall (Theorem 1.2) that the Lagrange interpolant \(p\in\mathbb P_m\) of \(f\in C^{m+1}[a,b]\) at distinct nodes \(x_0,\dots,x_m\) satisfies \[
f(x)-p(x)=\frac{f^{(m+1)}(\xi)}{(m+1)!}\prod_{i=0}^{m}(x-x_i),
\qquad
\norm{f-p}_{L^\infty[a,b]}\le\frac{\norm{f^{(m+1)}}_{L^\infty}}{(m+1)!}\,\norm{q_{\mathbf x}}_{L^\infty},
\tag{6.13}\] where \(q_{\mathbf x}(x)=\prod_{i=0}^m(x-x_i)\) is the node polynomial. The derivative factor is outside our control, but \(\norm{q_{\mathbf x}}_{L^\infty}\) is entirely ours to minimize through the node placement. For equispaced nodes it grows explosively near the ends — the Runge phenomenon of Figure 1.1. Since \(q_{\mathbf x}\) is monic of degree \(m+1\), Theorem 6.4 says it is made smallest by making it the monic Chebyshev polynomial \(\tilde T_{m+1}\), i.e. by taking the nodes to be the zeros of \(T_{m+1}\).






Figure 6.2: The monic node polynomial \(\prod_j(x-x_j)\) of degree \(10\) on \([-1,1]\) (vertical axis clipped to \(\pm0.04\)). With equispaced nodes it swings far outside the window near \(\pm1\); with Chebyshev nodes it is the monic \(\tilde T_{10}\), hugging zero with maximum modulus \(2^{-9}\).



Concretely, for \(m+1\) nodes we use the zeros Equation 6.10 of \(T_{m+1}\), \[
x_j=\cos\!\Bigl(\frac{2j+1}{2(m+1)}\pi\Bigr),\qquad j=0,\dots,m .
\] With this choice \(q_{\mathbf x}\) and \(\tilde T_{m+1}\) are both monic of degree \(m+1\) with the same zeros, hence equal, so \(\norm{q_{\mathbf x}}_{L^\infty}=2^{-m}\) (Figure 6.2). Substituting into Equation 6.13 gives a bound that shrinks with \(m\) instead of blowing up.

Theorem 6.5 (Chebyshev-node interpolation error) Let \(f\in C^{m+1}[-1,1]\) and let \(p\) be its Lagrange interpolant at the Chebyshev nodes \(x_j=\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{2j+1}{2(m+1)}\pi\bigr)\), \(j=0,\dots,m\). Then \[
\norm{f-p}_{L^\infty[-1,1]}\le\frac{\norm{f^{(m+1)}}_{L^\infty[-1,1]}}{2^{m}\,(m+1)!}.
\] On a general interval \([a,b]\), using the transported Chebyshev nodes, \[
\norm{f-p}_{L^\infty[a,b]}\le\Bigl(\frac{b-a}{2}\Bigr)^{m+1}\frac{\norm{f^{(m+1)}}_{L^\infty[a,b]}}{2^{m}\,(m+1)!}.
\]

The extra \(2^{-m}\) over the generic bound is exactly the equioscillation dividend. Choosing nodes well converts interpolation from a method that can diverge (equispaced) into one that converges geometrically for smooth \(f\) — the node-choice payoff promised in Chapter 1.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
section: 6.8 Gauss quadrature from the approximation viewpoint
text:
6.8 Gauss quadrature from the approximation viewpoint
Orthogonal polynomials also generate the optimal quadrature rules. Gauss quadrature approximates \(\int_a^b g(x)\,w(x)\,\dd x\approx\sum_{i=0}^{m}w_i\,g(x_i)\); from the present angle the nodes are the zeros of an orthogonal polynomial and the weights are integrals of Lagrange basis functions. This is the same rule established in Theorem 2.1, restated here for the weighted setting.

Theorem 6.6 (Gauss quadrature (approximation-theory form)) Let \(a<x_0<\dots<x_m<b\) be the zeros of \(p_{m+1}\), the degree-\((m+1)\) orthogonal polynomial for the weight \(w\) on \([a,b]\), and set \(\mathbf x=(x_0,\dots,x_m)\). Define the weights \[
w_i=\int_a^b L_i(x;\mathbf x)\,w(x)\,\dd x=\inner{L_i}{1}_w,\qquad i=0,\dots,m,
\] with \(L_i\) the Lagrange basis for the nodes \(\mathbf x\). Then \[
\int_a^b g(x)\,w(x)\,\dd x=\sum_{i=0}^{m}w_i\,g(x_i)\qquad\text{for all } g\in\mathbb P_{2m+1}.
\]

For \(g\in\mathbb P_m\) the rule is exact because \(g\) equals its own interpolant on \(m+1\) nodes; the striking part — exactness all the way to degree \(2m+1\) — holds only for the orthogonal-polynomial nodes (the proof is in Chapter 2). This is precisely the tool needed to run the projection Equation 6.6 in practice: the coefficients require inner products \(\inner{h_1}{h_2}_w=\int_a^b h_1 h_2\,w\,\dd x\), which we evaluate as \[
\inner{h_1}{h_2}_w\approx\sum_{i=0}^{m}w_i\,h_1(x_i)h_2(x_i) .
\] When \(h_1h_2\in\mathbb P_{2m+1}\) — as it is for the low-degree orthogonal polynomials themselves — this approximation is exact, a fact the next section exploits.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.9 Connecting interpolation and least-squares projection
Interpolation and least-squares projection look like different problems, yet Gauss quadrature makes them coincide at the right sampling rate. Define the approximate (discrete) inner product built from the Gauss nodes and weights for \(w\), \[
\inner{\phi}{\psi}_w^{(m)}\defeq\sum_{i=0}^{m}\phi(x_i)\,\psi(x_i)\,w_i .
\] Replacing the true inner products in Equation 6.6 by this discrete one gives a computable approximation to the least-squares-optimal \(p\). The surprise is what happens at \(m=n\).

Theorem 6.7 (Discrete projection equals interpolation) With the Gauss nodes and weights for \(w\) on \([a,b]\), let \[
q=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{\inner{f}{p_k}_w^{(n)}}{\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w^{(n)}}\,p_k\in\mathbb P_n .
\] Then \(q\) is exactly the Lagrange interpolant of \(f\) at the nodes \(x_0,\dots,x_n\); that is, \(q(x_i)=f(x_i)\) for all \(i\).


Proof. Let \(\ell\in\mathbb P_n\) be the Lagrange interpolant, \(\ell(x_i)=f(x_i)\). Three claims chain together.
Claim 1: \(\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w^{(n)}=\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w\) for \(k\le n\). Indeed \(p_k^2\in\mathbb P_{2n}\), on which the \((n{+}1)\)-node Gauss rule Theorem 6.6 is exact, so the discrete and true inner products agree.
Claim 2: \(\inner{f}{p_k}_w^{(n)}=\inner{\ell}{p_k}_w^{(n)}\). Both discrete inner products only see node values, and \(f(x_i)=\ell(x_i)\): \(\sum_i f(x_i)p_k(x_i)w_i=\sum_i\ell(x_i)p_k(x_i)w_i\).
Claim 3: \(\inner{\ell}{p_k}_w^{(n)}=\inner{\ell}{p_k}_w\). Since \(\ell p_k\in\mathbb P_{2n}\), exactness of the Gauss rule again equates the discrete and true inner products.
Combining, \(\inner{f}{p_k}_w^{(n)}=\inner{\ell}{p_k}_w\) and \(\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w^{(n)}=\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w\), so \[
q=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{\inner{\ell}{p_k}_w}{\inner{p_k}{p_k}_w}\,p_k ,
\] which by Theorem 6.2 is the exact least-squares projection of \(\ell\in\mathbb P_n\) onto \(\mathbb P_n\) — namely \(\ell\) itself. Hence \(q=\ell\). \(\square\)

Read the other way: Lagrange interpolation can be performed by a “lazy” evaluation of Gauss quadrature, which conveniently expresses the interpolant as a combination of orthogonal polynomials rather than of Lagrange basis functions — especially useful in the Chebyshev case, where it becomes a discrete cosine transform.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.10 Padé approximation
Polynomials are not always the best approximants; rational functions capture poles and asymptotes that polynomials cannot. A rational function of degree \(N\) is \(r=p/q\) with \(\deg p+\deg q=N\). Padé approximation is the rational analogue of the Taylor polynomial: match as many derivatives at a base point (taken to be \(x=0\)) as the free parameters allow.

Definition 6.4 (Padé approximant) Write \(p(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n}p_k x^{k}\) and \(q(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{m}q_k x^{k}\) (extend both coefficient sequences by zeros beyond their degrees). Normalizing \(q_0=1\) leaves \(n+m+1=N+1\) free parameters. The \([n/m]\) Padé approximant of \(f\) is the rational \(r=p/q\) chosen so that \[
r^{(k)}(0)=f^{(k)}(0),\qquad k=0,\dots,N .
\]

To construct it, expand \(f(x)=\sum_{k\ge0}a_k x^{k}\) (its Maclaurin series) and note \[
f-r=\frac{fq-p}{q},
\] so matching the first \(N\) derivatives of \(f-r\) at \(0\) is equivalent to killing the terms of order \(0,\dots,N\) in the numerator \(fq-p\). Expanding, \[
f(x)q(x)-p(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\sum_{i=0}^{k}a_i q_{k-i}-p_k\Bigr)x^{k},
\] so the conditions are the linear equations \[
p_k=\sum_{i=0}^{k}a_i q_{k-i},\qquad k=0,\dots,N,
\tag{6.14}\] which are \(N+1\) equations in the \(N+1\) unknowns \(p_0,\dots,p_n,q_1,\dots,q_m\). To cast Equation 6.14 as a matrix system, use \(q_0=1\) to write \(p_k-\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_{k-i}q_i=a_k\) and define the \((N+1)\times m\) matrix \(C=(C_{ki})\) (rows \(0,\dots,N\), columns \(1,\dots,m\)) with \(C_{ki}=a_{k-i}\) for \(i\le k\) and \(0\) otherwise, together with the \((N+1)\times(n+1)\) selector \(E=\bigl(\begin{smallmatrix}I_{n+1}\\ 0\end{smallmatrix}\bigr)\) picking out \(p_0,\dots,p_n\). Then, with \(\mathbf a=(a_0,\dots,a_N)^\top\), \(\mathbf p=(p_0,\dots,p_n)^\top\), \(\mathbf q=(q_1,\dots,q_m)^\top\), \[
E\mathbf p-C\mathbf q=\mathbf a
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix}E & -C\end{pmatrix}}_{=:A}\begin{pmatrix}\mathbf p\\ \mathbf q\end{pmatrix}=\mathbf a ,
\tag{6.15}\] an ordinary linear system for the coefficients. For instance, \(n=m=3\) (\(N=6\)) gives the \(7\times3\) lower-triangular Toeplitz \(C\) with rows \((0,0,0),(a_0,0,0),(a_1,a_0,0),\dots,(a_5,a_4,a_3)\).

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.11 Chebyshev rational approximation
Padé, like Taylor, is anchored at one point, so its quality is uneven across \([-1,1]\). For roughly uniform rational quality on \([-1,1]\) (reduce a general \([a,b]\) by the affine map of Section 6.5) replace the power-series expansions by Chebyshev expansions: \[
r(x)=\frac{\sum_{k=0}^{n}p_k T_k(x)}{\sum_{k=0}^{m}q_k T_k(x)},\qquad q_0=1,
\] and expand \(f(x)=\sum_{k\ge0}a_k T_k(x)\) with \(a_k=\inner{f}{T_k}_w/\inner{T_k}{T_k}_w\) under the Chebyshev weight \(w=1/\sqrt{1-x^2}\). The numerators \(\inner{f}{T_k}_w\) come from Gauss–Chebyshev quadrature (Section 6.8); the denominators are universal, \[
\inner{T_k}{T_k}_w=\begin{cases}\pi,&k=0,\\[2pt]\pi/2,&k\ge1.\end{cases}
\] As before we force the low-order terms of \(fq-p\) to vanish, but now \(T_iT_j\) is not a single Chebyshev polynomial. The saving identity (from homework) is \[
T_iT_j=\tfrac12\bigl[T_{i+j}+T_{\abs{i-j}}\bigr],
\tag{6.16}\] which lets the product \(fq\) be re-expanded in the \(T_k\). Collecting the coefficient of each \(T_k\) in \(fq-p\) and setting orders \(0,\dots,N\) to zero yields, after the bookkeeping, a linear system of the same shape as Equation 6.15: form the \((N+1)\times(m+1)\) matrices \(B^{(1)},B^{(2)},B^{(3)}\) with entries \(a_{k-i}\) (for \(i\le k\)), \(a_{i-k}\) (for \(i\ge k\)), and \(a_{i+k}\) (for \(k\ge1\)) respectively, and \(\tilde C=\tfrac12(B^{(1)}+B^{(2)}+B^{(3)})\), so that the conditions read \(E\mathbf p-\tilde C\tilde{\mathbf q}=0\). Splitting off the \(q_0=1\) column, \(\tilde C=(\mathbf c\ \ C)\) and \(\tilde{\mathbf q}=(1,\mathbf q^\top)^\top\), leaves \[
\begin{pmatrix}E & -C\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\mathbf p\\ \mathbf q\end{pmatrix}=\mathbf c ,
\] again an \(A\mathbf x=\mathbf c\) system. The three-term identity Equation 6.16 is the only new ingredient; everything else parallels Section 6.10.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.12 Fourier series
For periodic functions the natural basis is trigonometric. Consider \(f:[-\pi,\pi]\to\R\), extended \(2\pi\)-periodically to \(\R\) (\(f(x+2\pi k)=f(x)\)). Throughout this and the following sections \(i\) denotes the imaginary unit (\(i^2=-1\)). The functions \[
\phi_k(x)=\cos(kx)\ (k=0,\dots,n),\qquad \psi_k(x)=\sin(kx)\ (k=1,\dots,n)
\] are orthogonal under \(\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}=\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}_{L^2([-\pi,\pi])}\): all cross inner products vanish, and \[
\inner{\phi_0}{\phi_0}=2\pi,\qquad \inner{\phi_k}{\phi_k}=\inner{\psi_k}{\psi_k}=\pi\ (k\ge1),
\] verified as in Proposition 6.2.

Definition 6.5 (Trigonometric polynomials) \(\mathcal T_n\defeq\Span(\phi_0,\dots,\phi_n,\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\) is the space of trigonometric polynomials of degree \(\le n\); a member has degree \(n\) if the coefficient of \(\phi_n\) or \(\psi_n\) is nonzero.

Orthogonality makes the best \(\mathcal T_n\)-approximation another projection (Theorem 6.2 in trigonometric dress): \(\min_{\phi\in\mathcal T_n}\norm{\phi-f}^2\) is solved by \[
\phi(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bigl[a_k\cos(kx)+b_k\sin(kx)\bigr],
\tag{6.17}\] \[
a_k=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\cos(kx)\,\dd x\ (k\ge0),\qquad
b_k=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\sin(kx)\,\dd x\ (k\ge1) .
\] These are the truncated Fourier series coefficients, each \(a_k=\inner{f}{\phi_k}/\inner{\phi_k}{\phi_k}\) (the \(a_0/2\) absorbs the factor-of-two difference \(\inner{\phi_0}{\phi_0}=2\pi\)).

The complex exponential basis
The real basis is clarified by going complex. Extend the inner product to \(g,h:[-\pi,\pi]\to\C\) by \[
\inner{g}{h}=\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} g(x)\,\overline{h(x)}\,\dd x,
\] linear in the first slot with conjugate symmetry \(\inner{g}{h}=\overline{\inner{h}{g}}\) (hence conjugate-linear in the second). The exponentials \(e_k(x)=e^{ikx}\), \(k\in\Z\), are orthogonal: \[
\inner{e_k}{e_l}=\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{i(k-l)x}\,\dd x=2\pi\,\delta_{kl},
\] since for \(k\ne l\) the antiderivative \(e^{i(k-l)x}/[i(k-l)]\) is \(2\pi\)-periodic.

Theorem 6.8 (Real and complex trigonometric spans coincide) \(\mathcal T_n\subset\mathcal T_n^{\C}\defeq\Span_{\C}(\phi_0,\dots,\phi_n,\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)=\Span_{\C}(e_{-n},\dots,e_n)\).


Proof. \(\phi_0=e_0\), so it suffices to show \(\Span(\phi_k,\psi_k)=\Span(e_k,e_{-k})\) for \(k\ge1\). Euler’s formula \(e^{\pm ikx}=\cos(kx)\pm i\sin(kx)\) puts \(e_{\pm k}\in\Span(\phi_k,\psi_k)\); conversely \(\cos(kx)=(e^{ikx}+e^{-ikx})/2\) and \(\sin(kx)=(e^{ikx}-e^{-ikx})/(2i)\) put \(\phi_k,\psi_k\in\Span(e_k,e_{-k})\). \(\square\)

Consequently \(\min_{\phi\in\mathcal T_n^{\C}}\norm{\phi-f}^2\) is solved by \[
\phi(x)=\sum_{k=-n}^{n} c_k e^{ikx},\qquad c_k=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\,e^{-ikx}\,\dd x .
\tag{6.18}\] For real \(f\) the two minimizers agree, and the coefficients are related by \[
c_0=\frac{a_0}{2},\quad c_k=\frac{a_k-ib_k}{2},\quad c_{-k}=\frac{a_k+ib_k}{2},
\qquad a_k=c_k+c_{-k},\quad b_k=i(c_k-c_{-k}).
\]


Computing the coefficients: the trapezoidal rule
The integrals for \(a_k,b_k,c_k\) usually need numerical quadrature, and for periodic integrands the trapezoidal rule is the right choice. On the grid \(x_k=-\pi+kh\), \(h=2\pi/(M+1)\), \[
\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} g(x)\,\dd x\approx\frac h2\bigl[g(x_0)+g(x_{M+1})\bigr]+h\sum_{k=1}^{M}g(x_k),
\] which is exact for every trigonometric polynomial in \(\mathcal T_M\) — the periodic analogue of Gauss quadrature’s exactness on \(\mathbb P_{2M+1}\). When \(g\) is \(2\pi\)-periodic, \(g(x_0)=g(x_{M+1})\) and the rule collapses to the left-endpoint Riemann sum \(h\sum_{k=0}^{M}g(x_k)\), which is why the trapezoidal rule converges spectrally fast for smooth periodic integrands. Choosing \(M\gg n\) makes the coefficient errors negligible.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
section: 6.13 Discrete least squares with trigonometric polynomials
text:
6.13 Discrete least squares with trigonometric polynomials
In practice we often have only a sampling of a periodic \(f\) on the equispaced grid \[
x_j=-\pi+jh,\qquad j=0,\dots,2m-1,\qquad h=\frac{\pi}{m},
\] with \(y_j=f(x_j)\) (the point \(x_{2m}=\pi\) is redundant by periodicity). We fit these \(2m\) data points by the exponentials \(e_k(x)=e^{ikx}\) in discrete least squares. On the grid the exponentials alias: for all \(j\), \[
e_{k+2m}(x_j)=e^{ikx_j}\,e^{i\,2m(-\pi+j\pi/m)}=e^{ikx_j}\,e^{-2\pi i m}e^{2\pi i j}=e_k(x_j),
\] so more than \(2m\) consecutive frequencies are redundant. Taking \(k=-m,\dots,m-1\) gives \(2m\) parameters for \(2m\) points — enough to fit exactly. The normal equations \(A\mathbf c=\mathbf b\) use the discrete inner product \(A_{kl}=\sum_{j}\overline{e_k(x_j)}\,e_l(x_j)\), \(\mathbf b_k=\sum_j\overline{e_k(x_j)}\,y_j\). The matrix is a scaled identity: \[
A_{kl}=\sum_{j=0}^{2m-1}e^{i(l-k)x_j}=\sum_{j=0}^{2m-1}\Bigl(e^{\frac{2\pi i(l-k)}{2m}}\Bigr)^{j}=2m\,\delta_{kl},
\] because the geometric sum vanishes unless \(k-l\) is a multiple of \(2m\), which within \(-m,\dots,m-1\) happens only at \(k=l\). Hence \(A=2m\,I\) and \(\mathbf c=\tfrac{1}{2m}\mathbf b\): \[
c_k=\frac{e^{ik\pi}}{2m}\sum_{j=0}^{2m-1}e^{-\frac{2\pi i kj}{2m}}\,y_j,\qquad k=-m,\dots,m-1,
\tag{6.19}\] and the fit is \(\phi(x)=\sum_{k=-m}^{m-1}c_k e^{ikx}\). Zeroing the high-frequency \(c_k\) afterward recovers the least-squares fit in any smaller basis \(e_{-n},\dots,e_n\), \(n<m\). The whole cost sits in the sums Equation 6.19.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.14 The discrete Fourier transform
The sums in Equation 6.19 are a discrete Fourier transform (DFT). First clear the negative indices using the aliasing \(e_{k+2m}=e_k\): define, for \(k=0,\dots,2m-1\), \[
\tilde c_k=\frac{e^{ik\pi}}{2m}\sum_{j=0}^{2m-1}e^{-\frac{2\pi i kj}{2m}}\,y_j,
\] and recover the wanted coefficients by stacking the last \(m\) entries on top: \(c_0=\tilde c_0,\dots,c_{m-1}=\tilde c_{m-1}\) and \(c_{-m}=\tilde c_m,\dots,c_{-1}=\tilde c_{2m-1}\). Peeling off the prefactor, define \[
\hat y_k=\sum_{j=0}^{2m-1}e^{-\frac{2\pi i kj}{2m}}\,y_j,\qquad k=0,\dots,2m-1,
\tag{6.20}\] so \(\tilde c_k=\tfrac{e^{ik\pi}}{2m}\hat y_k\). With \(N=2m\), Equation 6.20 is the matrix–vector product in the following definition.

Definition 6.6 (Discrete Fourier transform) The DFT of \(y\in\C^{N}\) is \(\hat y=\mathbf F y\), where \(\mathbf F=(F_{kl})\) is the \(N\times N\) matrix with zero-indexed entries \[
F_{kl}=e^{-\frac{2\pi i kl}{N}} .
\]

The entire pipeline for discrete trigonometric least squares is now: (i) form \(\hat y=\mathbf F y\); (ii) set \(\tilde c_k=\tfrac{e^{ik\pi}}{2m}\hat y_k\); (iii) restack to get \(\mathbf c\); (iv) read off \(\phi(x)=\sum_{k=-n}^{n}c_k e^{ikx}\). A naïve matvec by \(\mathbf F\) costs \(\bigO(N^2)\) — which the next section removes.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.15 The fast Fourier transform
Forming \(\mathbf F\) and multiplying costs \(\bigO(N^2)\), but the DFT never needs the explicit matrix: the fast Fourier transform (FFT) computes \(\hat y=\mathbf F^{(N)}y\) in \(\bigO(N\log N)\). It is, plausibly, the most important numerical algorithm ever devised — known to Gauss in 1805 and rediscovered by Cooley and Tukey in 1965. Whenever periodic functions, trigonometric polynomials, or Fourier coefficients appear, the goal is to reduce the computation to the right FFTs.
Assume \(N=2^{n}\) (a general \(N\) reduces to this case). Split the DFT sum Equation 6.20 into even- and odd-indexed terms, writing \(\omega_N=e^{-2\pi i/N}\) and using \(\omega_N^{2}=\omega_{N/2}\): \[
\hat y_k=\sum_{l=0}^{N-1}\omega_N^{\,kl}y_l
=\sum_{r=0}^{N/2-1}\omega_{N/2}^{\,kr}y_{2r}
+\omega_N^{\,k}\sum_{r=0}^{N/2-1}\omega_{N/2}^{\,kr}y_{2r+1}.
\] Each sum is a DFT of length \(N/2\) — on the even part \(y^{(e)}\) and the odd part \(y^{(o)}\). Let \(\mathbf D^{(N)}=\diag(\omega_N^{0},\dots,\omega_N^{N/2-1})\) be the twiddle factors and set \(u=\mathbf F^{(N/2)}y^{(e)}\), \(v=\mathbf D^{(N)}\mathbf F^{(N/2)}y^{(o)}\). Because the length-\(N/2\) transforms are \(N/2\)-periodic in \(k\), the two halves of \(\hat y\) share these vectors: \[
\hat y_k=u_k+v_k,\qquad \hat y_{k+N/2}=u_k-v_k,\qquad k=0,\dots,N/2-1 .
\tag{6.21}\] The \(\pm\) pair Equation 6.21 is the butterfly: two half-size DFTs plus one diagonal matvec and \(N\) additions.






NoteAlgorithm — recursive radix-2 FFT



To compute \(\hat y=\mathbf F^{(N)}y\):

If \(N=1\), return \(\hat y=y\).
Otherwise split \(y\) into its even part \(y^{(e)}\) and odd part \(y^{(o)}\).
Recursively compute \(u=\mathbf F^{(N/2)}y^{(e)}\) and \(v=\mathbf D^{(N)}\bigl[\mathbf F^{(N/2)}y^{(o)}\bigr]\).
Return \(\hat y=\bigl(\begin{smallmatrix}u+v\\ u-v\end{smallmatrix}\bigr)\).



Each call does \(\bigO(N)\) work outside its recursive calls. Summed over the recursion tree there are \(1\) call of size \(N\), \(2\) of size \(N/2\), \(4\) of size \(N/4\), and so on — \(\bigO(N)\) work at each of the \(\log_2 N+1\) levels — for a total of \(\bigO(N\log N)\). That speedup is what makes Fourier methods, spectral differentiation, and fast convolution practical.

title: 6  Approximation Theory
text:
6.16 Chapter summary

Best approximation in an inner-product norm is orthogonal projection. Discrete least squares reduces to the normal equations \(G^\top G\mathbf a=G^\top y\) (Theorem 6.1); over an interval it becomes the Gram-matrix system Equation 6.4 with \(L^2\) (or weighted-\(L^2\)) inner products.
Choosing an orthogonal polynomial basis (Definition 6.2) diagonalizes the Gram matrix, so the projection is the term-by-term formula Equation 6.6. The basis is built by Gram–Schmidt

and evaluated stably by the three-term recurrence Equation 6.7, never through its expanded coefficients.

Chebyshev polynomials \(T_n(x)=\cos(n\arccos x)\) (Definition 6.3) equioscillate on \([-1,1]\), are orthogonal for \(w=1/\sqrt{1-x^2}\), and their monic versions are the minimax-flattest monic polynomials (Theorem 6.4). This drives economization Equation 6.12 and, by minimizing the node polynomial, makes Chebyshev nodes the cure for the Runge phenomenon (Theorem 6.5, Figure 6.2, Figure 1.1).
Gauss quadrature (Theorem 6.6, cf. Theorem 2.1) uses orthogonal-polynomial zeros as nodes and is exact on \(\mathbb P_{2m+1}\); sampling the least-squares projection at \(n+1\) Gauss nodes reproduces the Lagrange interpolant exactly (Theorem 6.7).
Rational approximation: Padé (Definition 6.4) matches derivatives at a point via the linear system Equation 6.15; Chebyshev rational approximation trades the power series for a Chebyshev expansion, using \(T_iT_j=\tfrac12[T_{i+j}+T_{\abs{i-j}}]\), for uniform quality on \([-1,1]\).
Fourier series project onto trigonometric polynomials (Definition 6.5); the complex exponentials \(e^{ikx}\) (Theorem 6.8) give the cleanest basis. Discrete trigonometric least squares has the scaled-identity normal matrix \(A=2mI\), reducing to a DFT (Definition 6.6), which the FFT evaluates in \(\bigO(N\log N)\) via the butterfly Equation 6.21.

title: 7  Eigenvalue Problems
text:
7.1 The power method
Eigenvalues and eigenvectors are constructed in linear-algebra courses through the characteristic polynomial \(\det(A-\lambda I)=0\), but that route is useless numerically: root-finding on a degree-\(n\) polynomial is unstable, and a full diagonalization costs \(\bigO(n^3)\). In practice one rarely wants the whole spectrum. A handful of extreme eigenpairs already answers the questions that matter — the stationary distribution of a Markov chain, the PageRank of a web graph, the leading directions of a data cloud, a low-rank compression of a matrix, a clustering of a point set. This chapter builds the tools that extract those few eigenpairs cheaply, using nothing but repeated matrix–vector products. We start from the simplest such idea, the power method, and grow it into subspace iteration, the randomized SVD, and spectral clustering.
Throughout, \(A\in\R^{n\times n}\) has eigenvalues \(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n\) with eigenvectors \(v_1,\dots,v_n\); \(\spec(A)=\max_i\abs{\lambda_i}\) is the spectral radius (Definition 5.4), and \(\sigma_1\ge\sigma_2\ge\dots\) are singular values. We freely reuse the norm and condition-number conventions of Notation.
The power method is the simplest iterative method for an eigenvector: it constructs the dominant eigenvector as the limit of a sequence of vectors. It is also the simplest matrix-free method, meaning it needs only the ability to compute \(Au\) for a given \(u\) — never the entries of \(A\). This matters because a matrix–vector product (“matvec”) is often far cheaper than forming \(A\): a sparse or “sparse-plus-low-rank” \(A\) costs \(\bigO(n)\) per matvec, so a few eigenpairs can be had in \(\bigO(n)\) time, against the \(\bigO(n^3)\) of a full diagonalization.

title: 7  Eigenvalue Problems
text:
The idea and why it converges
Start from any vector \(x\in\R^n\) and look at the sequence \(Ax, A^2x, A^3x,\dots\). On its own this usually either blows up or collapses to zero, so it does not converge; but if we renormalize at each step, the direction settles down. Suppose \(A\) is diagonalizable with a strictly dominant eigenpair \((\lambda_1,v_1)\), meaning \[
\abs{\lambda_1}>\abs{\lambda_2}\ge\dots\ge\abs{\lambda_n}.
\tag{7.1}\] (If \(A\) is real, the dominant \(\lambda_1\) must itself be real, since complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs of equal modulus.) Expand the start vector in the eigenbasis, \(x=\sum_{i=1}^n c_iv_i\). Then \[
A^kx=\sum_{i=1}^n c_i\lambda_i^k v_i,
\qquad
\lambda_1^{-k}A^kx=\sum_{i=1}^n c_i\Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_1}\Bigr)^k v_i .
\] Every ratio \(\abs{\lambda_i/\lambda_1}<1\) for \(i>1\), so those terms vanish geometrically and \(\lambda_1^{-k}A^kx\to c_1v_1\). The iteration finds \(v_1\) — provided \(c_1\ne0\), i.e. the start vector has a nonzero component along \(v_1\) (a random \(x\) satisfies this with probability one).
We do not know \(\lambda_1\), so we cannot form \(\lambda_1^{-k}A^kx\); instead we simply normalize to keep the iterate at unit length. Define \[
u^{(k)}\defeq\frac{A^kx}{\ntwo{A^kx}} .
\tag{7.2}\] Factoring out the dominant term, \[
u^{(k)}=\frac{\sum_i c_i\lambda_i^k v_i}{\bigl\lVert\sum_i c_i\lambda_i^k v_i\bigr\rVert}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\underbrace{\frac{c_1}{\abs{c_1}}\Bigl(\frac{\lambda_1}{\abs{\lambda_1}}\Bigr)^k}_{=:z^{(k)}}
\,\frac{v_1}{\ntwo{v_1}},
\] where \(\abs{z^{(k)}}=1\) (in the real case \(z^{(k)}=\pm1\)). The unit scalar \(z^{(k)}\) records an unavoidable phase/sign ambiguity — an eigenvector is only defined up to scaling.

Theorem 7.1 (Power method convergence) Let \(A\in\R^{n\times n}\) be diagonalizable with a strictly dominant eigenpair \((\lambda_1,v_1)\) as in Equation 7.1, and let \(x=\sum_i c_iv_i\) with \(c_1\ne0\). With \(u^{(k)}\) as in Equation 7.2 there exist unit scalars \(z^{(k)}\) (\(z^{(k)}=\pm1\) when \(A\) is real) such that \[
\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{u^{(k)}}{z^{(k)}}=\frac{v_1}{\ntwo{v_1}} .
\] The convergence is geometric: the error decays like \(\bigl(\abs{\lambda_2}/\abs{\lambda_1}\bigr)^k\), governed by the spectral gap \(\abs{\lambda_1}-\abs{\lambda_2}\).


Proof. The displayed limit is the computation above: \(\lambda_1^{-k}A^kx\to c_1v_1\) term by term, and normalizing removes the divergent scalar \(\lambda_1^k\) while leaving the unit phase \(z^{(k)}\). The rate is set by the largest surviving ratio, \(\abs{\lambda_2/\lambda_1}\), since that term dominates the deviation \(u^{(k)}-z^{(k)}v_1/\ntwo{v_1}\). \(\square\)

The rate claim is exactly what Figure 7.1 shows: a spectral gap ratio of \(0.5\) gains roughly one digit every two steps, while a ratio of \(0.9\) crawls. The method also works when \(A\) is not diagonalizable, as long as a strictly dominant eigenvector exists; the proof is more delicate and goes through the Jordan normal form.






Figure 7.1: Alignment error \(\sqrt{1-(u^{(k)}\cdot v_1)^2}\) of the power method on a symmetric \(4\times4\) matrix, for two spectra. The decay is geometric with rate \(\abs{\lambda_2/\lambda_1}\) (dashed reference lines); a small spectral gap makes the method slow.





Estimating the eigenvalue and stopping
To terminate we want a residual, ideally the relative residual norm \(\ntwo{Au^{(k)}-\lambda_1u^{(k)}}/\lambda_1\) — but that needs \(\lambda_1\). Given an eigenvector guess \(u\) we recover the matching eigenvalue by the Rayleigh quotient: if \(Au=\lambda u\) then \(u\cdot(Au)=\lambda\ntwo{u}^2\), so \[
\lambda=\frac{u^\top Au}{\ntwo{u}^2},
\qquad\text{and for unit }u,\quad \lambda=u^\top Au .
\tag{7.3}\] This furnishes the running estimate \(\lambda_1^{(k)}=u^{(k)}\cdot(Au^{(k)})\) and the computable stopping test \(\ntwo{Au^{(k)}-\lambda_1^{(k)}u^{(k)}}/\lambda_1^{(k)}\le\eps\).






NoteAlgorithm — Power method



Given a start vector \(x^{(0)}\in\R^n\), a matvec routine for \(A\), a maximum count \(m\), and a tolerance \(\eps\). For \(k=0,1,\dots,m\):

Normalize: \(u^{(k)}\leftarrow x^{(k)}/\ntwo{x^{(k)}}\).
Matvec: \(x^{(k+1)}\leftarrow Au^{(k)}\).
Rayleigh-quotient estimate: \(\lambda_1^{(k)}\leftarrow u^{(k)}\cdot x^{(k+1)}\).
If \(\ntwo{x^{(k+1)}-\lambda_1^{(k)}u^{(k)}}/\lambda_1^{(k)}\le\eps\), break.

Return \((\lambda_1^{(k)},u^{(k)})\). Each step is one matvec plus \(\bigO(n)\) work.


The power method generalizes, from one dominant eigenvector to the \(k\) most dominant ones, as subspace iteration (Section 7.6). First we spend three sections on where the dominant eigenvector of a specific matrix is exactly the quantity of interest.

title: 7  Eigenvalue Problems
text:
7.2 Application: Markov chains
Consider a system with \(n\) states \(1,\dots,n\) making random transitions at discrete times. For each state \(j\) we are given a column of probabilities \(p_j=(p_{1j},\dots,p_{nj})^\top\), where \(p_{ij}\) is the probability of moving to state \(i\) next, given that we are in state \(j\) now — transitions depend only on the current state (the Markov property). For these to be probabilities we need \(p_{ij}\ge0\) and \(\sum_i p_{ij}=1\) for every \(j\); the latter reads \(\mathbf 1^\top p_j=1\) with \(\mathbf 1\) the all-ones vector.

Definition 7.1 (Column-stochastic matrix) \(P=(p_{ij})\) is (column) stochastic if \(P\ge0\) entrywise and \(P^\top\mathbf 1=\mathbf 1\) (every column sums to \(1\)). It is positive, written \(P>0\), if \(p_{ij}>0\) for all \(i,j\).

A vector \(\pi\in\R^n\) is a probability distribution over the states if \(\pi\ge0\) and \(\mathbf 1^\top\pi=1\). The point of the stochastic matrix is that it advances distributions in time.

Proposition 7.1 (Distributions evolve by \(P\)) If \(\pi\) is the distribution of the state \(X_t\) at time \(t\), then \(P\pi\) is its distribution at time \(t+1\).


Proof. Conditioning on the current state and using the Markov property, \[
\Pr(X_{t+1}=i)=\sum_{j=1}^n\Pr(X_{t+1}=i\mid X_t=j)\,\Pr(X_t=j)=\sum_{j=1}^n p_{ij}\pi_j=[P\pi]_i .
